metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_1142
metadata.comment:
Rationale: In this paper, McEwen investigate the role of tomosyn-1 in C. elegans mutants lacking the protein (TOMO-1 mut). McEwen et al observe increased Ach secretion at tomo-1 mut body muscles by recording EPSCs. The authors find that stimulus-evoked EPSC amplitudes and total synaptic charge transfer are significantly increased in mutants (Fig. 1 A-D). This results suggest that tomosyn acts as inhibitor of synaptic transmission. In this line of experiments, by measuring of hypertonic sucrose evoked response, the authors show more than 400% increase in EPSC and total synaptic charge transfer, indicating that the increased secretion of Ach in tomo-1 mut is caused by increase in the pool of primed SVs (fig. 4A,B)

These results are consistent with the reports mentioned in the additional literature, showing that overexpression of tomosyn in chromaffin cells reduces the number of primed vesicles (see Hatsuzawa 2003, Gracheva 2006 and Yizhar 2004).. Experimental description: Introduction and materials&methods
Characterization of Additional Splice Forms of tomo-1
During cloning of tomo-1,we discovered two additional splice forms
of tomo-1 that were not described in Wormbase or represented in
EST libraries.
Immunolabeling, Subcellular Fractionation,
and Coimmunoprecipitations
For immunolabeling experiments, wild-type and tomo-1(nu468)
worms were fixed using Bouin's fixative (Nonet et al., 1997) and la-
beled with either antibodies to UNC-64 Syntaxin (1:500), RIC-4
SNAP-25 (1:500), or UNC-10 RIM1 (1:50,000) (Koushika et al., 2001;
Saifee et al., 1998; Weimer et al., 2003).
